mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Range Rover Sport 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 306DT engine typically costs £6,500–£9,500 supply only, with used units available from £3,500–£5,500. Fitting adds £2,000–£3,000 depending on the workshop. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your Range Rover Sport.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Range Rover Sport 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on maintenance history. The 306DT has a known Achilles' heel: the crankshaft bearing. With 5,000-mile oil changes, the risk is reduced but not eliminated. With the standard 16,000-mile JLR service interval, bearing failure at 80,000–140,000 miles is almost inevitable. Buy on history.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Land Rover Range Rover Sport 3.0 TDV6/SDV6?

acceptedAnswer.text: Crankshaft bearing failure (catastrophic), turbocharger failure (often linked to oil starvation), and EGR/DPF clogging. The crankshaft bearing is the single most critical issue — prevent it with frequent oil changes and immediate investigation of any cold knock.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Range Rover Sport 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Range Rover Sport values remain strong — a well-kept L494 is often worth £12,000–£30,000+. A £6,500–£9,500 reconditioned engine is 30–50% of the vehicle's value — apply the Land Rover 60% scrap threshold. If the chassis and body are good, replacement is often worth it.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Land Rover Range Rover Sport 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is 306DT — it's the only 3.0-litre V6 diesel used in the Range Rover Sport. You can find it on the VIN plate, in the vehicle documentation, or on a label on the engine itself. Production years: L320 (2009–2013) and L494 (2013–2020).

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the 306DT crankshaft bearing, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: It can be repaired — but only if caught early. A bottom-end rebuild with new bearing shells and a crankshaft polish/regrind costs £5,500–£7,000. If the bearing has failed catastrophically and scored the crankshaft or block, a full engine replacement (£6,500+) is usually required. The only reliable prevention is 5,000-mile oil changes and immediate investigation of any cold-start knock.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current Range Rover Sport 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 market. This badge spans two generations — see the Era Map above for generation‑specific pricing. The L494 typically commands a premium for lower-mileage examples.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Range Rover Sport 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 engine typically takes 15–20 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer).

problems.title: Crankshaft Bearing Failure (The "Crankshaft Killer")
problems.symptoms: Loud knocking from the bottom end on cold start (lasting 3–10+ seconds), metallic ticking that worsens as the engine warms, low oil pressure warning, sudden catastrophic failure, engine seizes while driving
problems.repairCost: £5,500–£7,000 (bottom-end rebuild with new bearings, crankshaft polish/regrind)
problems.replacementCost: £6,500–£9,500 (if crankshaft is scored or rod/piston damage has occurred)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: any bottom-end knock on a 306DT requires immediate investigation and likely engine-out work

problems.recommendation: This is the single most infamous failure in the modern Land Rover range. The tin-aluminium bearing shells have a coating that fatigues over time — once it's gone, the bearing fails, and the crankshaft scores the block. The only reliable prevention is 5,000-mile oil changes (not the 16,000-mile JLR interval) and immediate investigation of any cold-start knock. If you hear cold knock that lasts more than a second or two, stop driving and have the vehicle recovered. A bottom-end rebuild (£5,500–£7,000) is significantly cheaper than a full engine replacement (£6,500+).

failureLink.label: Read the full crankshaft bearing failure guide

problems.title: Turbocharger Failure (Oil Starvation)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, excessive black or blue smoke, whistling or grinding noise from the engine bay, engine warning light
problems.recommendation: Turbocharger failure on the 306DT is often linked to the same issue that kills the crankshaft bearings — oil starvation. If the oil pump can't maintain pressure, the turbo suffers too. A failing turbo can send debris into the engine, causing further damage. If you notice a whistling or grinding noise, or any loss of power, get it checked immediately. A replacement turbo is a straightforward job for a JLR specialist.

failureLink.label: Read the full turbocharger failure guide

problems.title: EGR Valve and DPF Clogging
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, hesitation on acceleration, reduced fuel economy, engine warning light, DPF regeneration warning
problems.recommendation: Like all modern diesels, the 306DT suffers from EGR and DPF issues, particularly if the vehicle is used for short journeys. Carbon build-up in the EGR system and DPF clogging are common at higher mileages. Regular motorway driving (to allow DPF regeneration) and occasional EGR cleaning can extend the life of these components. If the DPF warning light appears, take the car for a 20–30 minute motorway run.

failureLink.label: Read the full EGR and DPF failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Range Rover Sport 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Bottom-end rebuild with new bearings and crankshaft polish/regrind
If the crankshaft is scored or the block is damaged, replacement is the only option
Straightforward replacement — check oil feed pipe
Simple cleaning or replacement — restores performance
Forced regeneration or replacement; prevention is key

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Range Rover Sport 3.0 TDV6/SDV6? Check These First
buyIf: * Proven oil change history at 5,000–8,000 mile intervals — this is the single most critical check on the 306DT
* No cold-start knock — the defining risk of this engine; any bottom-end noise is a red flag
* Full service history with correct oil spec (5W‑30 or 0W‑30, always meeting Ford/LR spec)
* Evidence of turbo health check and oil feed pipe inspection
avoidIf: * Any cold-start knock — walk away unless you're budgeting for a full engine replacement
* Poor service history or evidence of extended oil change intervals — the 306DT is unforgiving of neglect
* Evidence of repeated oil top-ups without a diagnosed leak — this often indicates bearing wear or turbo issues
* Any engine warning light without a clear diagnosis — the 306DT is complex and expensive to diagnose

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 306DT is a powerful and torquey engine that makes the Range Rover Sport a truly capable vehicle. But it has one catastrophic weakness: the crankshaft bearing. Short oil change intervals (5,000 miles, not 16,000) and immediate investigation of any cold-start knock are the only defences. If you find a car with a documented history of frequent oil changes and no bottom-end noise, the engine can be reliable. But the risk is real — budget for a bottom-end rebuild or replacement at around 100,000 miles as a preventive measure.

cta.label: → Compare 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover Sport 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Any bottom-end knock lasting more than 1–2 seconds? This is the single most critical check on the 306DT
2. Service History — including which generation (L320 or L494) and evidence of 5,000–8,000 mile oil changes
3. Oil Pressure — Any low oil pressure warnings? (bearing wear warning)
4. Turbo Health — Any whistling, grinding, or blue smoke under acceleration?
5. DPF History — Check for regeneration issues; short journeys accelerate DPF wear
6. Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles? (bearing or turbo warning)
7. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures (EGR, DPF, or catalyst issues)

If the bearing has failed catastrophically and scored the crankshaft or block, a full engine replacement (£6,500+) is usually required. The only reliable prevention is 5,000-mile oil changes and immediate investigation of any cold-start knock.
